headlines.title: See America First
headlines.fullText:
"See America first" has been for some years a slogan of patriotic Intent, and current events and conditiona across the seas tend greatly to accentuate Its Importance and relevancy. The problem of get- ting our thousands of globe-trottera home again and down to a moderate and orderly pace upon their own native heaths has been one of the most serious and perplexing the government and banking cir- cles have had to deal with. How to ship some mil- lions of dollars in world-current gold coin to refresh the vacuous pockets of our ettizens and citizenetten Inclined to potter around through the historie ruina, tar-spring sections and art centers of the Old World. suddenly grew into a vexatious puzzle productive of more or less gooseflesh to publie authorities and fiscal agencies of the New. And even now that the gold has gone across, and the distracted tourists can again feast their hungry eyes upon the heartening sight of Uncle Sam's favorite pet eagle on Its own aureate nest, additional troubles and delays in transit supervene on account of lack of ships to fetch the penitent prodigals back to their father's house and to the fatted American calf at a dollar a throw for T-bone steak.
But this feature of the difficult and urgent task of repatriation will no doubt be overcome also and our thousands of summer adventurers into lands of fair delight will come trooping home full of thrilling experiences and of stories of the horrors of war hardly fit to tell even to the marines.
After allowing for the additional chances of war and weather on land and sea, long before the crim- son caput of the omnipresent American Thanksgiving gobbler falls across the block, our thoroughly repa- triated brothers and sisters should all be "in our midst" once more, safe, happy and, it is to be hoped for many of them, "cured." Of course, to see the world Is not only a pleasure but an achievement, but the world is a rather large establishment, and persons of average mind and modest means should consider themselves fairly well rewarded by seeing one side of it only, especially since the American or home side is vastly rich in nature's wonders which are better to look at than the ruins of old castlea and middle-aged dukes in Europe.
Por the majority of those who annually strain their financial resources to visit Europe and their minds in trying to size the Old World up In three weeks, "See America first" In an excellent and ap- propriate shibboleth, and should now be painted larger and more Impressively across the wander-lust perspective of the plain people.
Better le a shady creek bank at home, with plenty bait in the minnow bucket and a jug of grape juice anchored in the near-by spring, than the most glo- rious prospect of the Alpa or Apennines that bristles with bayonets and spits fire day and night. "See America first, last and all the time" would be a good motto for every American family with only one cow.

headlines.title: Teaching the Old Idea How to Shoot
Cracks at the Crowd
headlines.subtitle: BY CLAUDE CALLAN.
headlines.fullText:
If We were just forced to go te wir-with a long, glittering bar. onet at our back-we would much prefer meeting soldiers who are not so determined as those in En- rope. They fight too seriously. Frequently they don't even know when they are whipped, and go right on fighting, as it they enjoyed it. We would prefer fighting soldiers who went about their business just as a fellow does who is working by the day.
There is no occasion for overdo- ing anything. If we had to be in a battle we would want the armles to be about five miles from each other.
In these contesta where one is marched right up to the muzzle of the enemy's guns we would be too nervous to be of much assistance. We are as much afraid of a gun barrel as a lady is of a mouse. And speaking of mice-they may play an important part In warfare when women have their rights, and are permitted to win glory-or go to it-in the field. An opposing army could load Its cannon with mice, fire them at the ladies, and no officer could rally the deur women from the pante. If they had lady officers there would be no attempt made. The officers would be a part of the pante. Personally, we wouldn't be afraid to face an army of women-unless we bad a wife in the ranks A lady is a very poor shot except when she has her husband for a target.
When women have charge of the government they probably will have all the married ladies remain at home to look after their work and provide for the husbands. This will place none but single women - In the field, and they would make a poor showing against an army of men. Even the girls who were blessed with beaux would be slow to fire on the enemy. They would realize that the time might come when they would want second husbands.
And the dear bachelor girls-those who have reach4 -A twenty years and gult aging-could never be pas sunded to shoot a man. They might be willing to wound one, and then bind up his wounds. It per- haps would please them to surround the army of men, and lead them in captivity, to the army chap- lain.
But no amount of persuasion could induce an elderly young lady to send anything worse than & Cupid shaft Into the heart of man. Of course, after one was married a few years she might feel called upon to take down the army musket and go after husband.
The Democrats would have -he cost of living down to nearly nothing if the Republicana hadn't started that war in Europe.
Mose Harris, major and editor of the Texas Re- publie, says that President Wilson should person- ally pay John Lind for his silence in Mexico. Major Mone is a Progressive and he thinks a fellow tan't earning his wages unless he talks as much as Colonel T. R. does.
When women control the nations France will rule the world. If a country refuses to recognize her the Hlaht mistress of styles she can cut off its supply and force the earth into submission.
If America can get hold of some nice second-hand ships she is certain to appropriate much Europea trade to her own use and benefit while those coun- tries are pelting each other. Of course, when peace comes those nations may get together and command Uncle Sam to quit infringing on thetr territory with his chewing gum, steam engines, knitting needles, agricultural Implements and other household neces- sities. They may contend that Samuel took an un- fair advantage while they were buny thinning their population down to a stand. Being a sensible peo- ple we naturally prefer peace, and if those powers should Would place commence in making hip pocket movements it us an awful position. Uncle Sam couldn't afford to go to war just for the sake of selling a few grindstones, cream separators and monkey wrenches in foreign but still it would be provoking to have other countries feel that they bluffed un. We are in hopes that Europe wil be reasonable in the matter and not start a funs. The opening up of new territory makes it necessary for the American people to be very careful in the matter of selling on time. It will not do to open an account with every man one mees in foreign countries. But our experience has been that the merchanta will not be careless in this matter. In fact, the most of them we have had dealings with seem to be too ready to turn a fellow down. If he fails to pay them one bill they remember it against him forever, At least, we have found it that way.
An English lady says that society in Europe is affected by the war. Well, that is too horrible for anything, and they ought to quit fighting. It is ter- rible to think of sending a woman's husband to the field, but when she must allow her new dresses to go unworn because there are no social affairs it is time to call a halt.
A big shipment of Democratic money has reached Texas, and bankers should not lend a dollar to any but faithful soldiers in the righteous army that brought political redemption to the nation.
A writer suggests having a separate house-a playhouse-for the children. It would be nice to get all the noise removed from the mansion proper, but it will be time enough for us to consider the matter when we get the mortgage off our one roof. So far it is sticking to the shingles like a porous plaster.
The military experts in the United States are just as numerous as the male population, and possibly a few suffragettes are explaining how it should be done.
Even soap has advanced in price. It is gratifying to learn that the war has at least brought gladness to the hearts of the children in this country.
The Bryan Eagle says that "every man who has a patch of ground as big as a barrel head should grow something on it." That Is just about the sime farm it would quit most people to cultivate-especially during the summer months.
Even dry goods are advancing. This indicates that supply and demand has nothing to do with prices. Certainly there are less dry goods being used than at any time in the past. A lady's dress now doesn't require half so much cloth as one sleeve did twenty years ago.
This from the Ballinger Democrat: "What is the use of Germany getting Hungary and starving when there is another of the horrible results of war. it does seem that those foolish nationa would see, the error of their way.

headlines.title: Across the Rio Grande
headlines.fullText:
Present Indications are that the administration's policy in dealing with Mexico has been completely Vindicated. It now seems almost certain that there revolutionary trouble, although that trouble may con- tinue in spite of the triumph of Carranza.
The policy of this government during the Huerta rule was not due to the civil war In Mexico, but to the fact that Huerta reached the presidency hy nieans that could not be approved. On this account recornition by the United States was denied. This policy aided the constitutionallata and assured their final triumph.
In view of the fact that Carranza has reached the provisional presidency by proper means, there will be no casse for the United States to withhold recog- Biston, iz it appears that a stable government can be set up and maintained. With this recognition the Mexican government will be able to secure credit. and will therefore be in better position to face oppo- sition from any source.
But whatever the final outcome, it is not likely that the United States will find cause to Intervene so long as the lives and property of Americans are not endangered. The spirit of friendship and human- Ity canses all citizens of this country to hope that permanent peace will come to that nation without further bloodshed, but it is gratifying to know that there Is little danger of becoming involved in a war.
So far as Mexico is concerned It Is certain that many are displeased with the turn of affairs. Villa is not likely to relish what he must consider im- proper treatment, and instead of supporting the tri- umphant Carranza It is probable that he will be ready to continue the revolution.
The strength of any opposition to the present gov. ernment will be determined, in a measure at least, nce received from the outside. When the ninews of war cease to flow into the re- public there will be a greater sentiment for peace on the part of those who are fighting the battles. So long as an army, composed of men who are nome too willing to work, is furnished with proper supplies, the men will be ready to face the dangers, but when they are forced to face hardships even greater than that of labor, In addition to the dangers, they will not be slow to tire of the confilet.
Mexico has great problems to solve. Even with permanent peace she has questions that statesmen must contend with for many years. Her people must be taught to make proper use of the soil, and there must be measures having for their object an Increase in amall home owners. This country has a problem of the same character, but ita solution is atmple when compared with that of Mexico.
The end of war will not mean the end of trouble for that republic. It will be only the beginning of advancement. But since even the beginning cannot be made amill peace ja restored it is highly impor- fant that those people realize the folly of internal strife at the earilest pourtore moment
The very fact that peace will not bring with It a solution of the problems confronting the mantes prove a cauas for future strife. The people may expect too much of even a wise and just adminis- tration of the nation's affairs. Unakilled In the art of government they may think that a few months should place them in a position to settle down in comfortable homes. But miracles cannot be worked In the affairs of state, elther in Mexico or in America. There and here the individual must work for all that he can call his own. The government can make just laws, giving every man an opportunity, but the man himself must improve that opportunity.
Mexico at peace, with the country developed, will mean much to the United States, and especially to Texas. This alone would be enough to cause Amer- teans to hope that her wars are over, but even if she is not yet ready to lay down the rifle and take up the plow, we can rejoice that this country will not be called upon to sacrifice its moms in that republic.

headlines.title: Party Loyalty
headlines.subtitle: BY GEORGE FITCH, Author of "At Good Old Siwash."
headlines.fullText:
Party loyalty is the glue which binds the voter to the politician. It is the chief stock-in-trade of the latter. Without party loyalty he would be as help- leas as a mason without mortar. Whenever a poli- tician's voters drop off of him he is a gone gosling and has to spend the rest of his life working. There- fore party loyalty means more to the politician than the ten commandments or the constitution or Rob- erts' rule of order. He can get along without all of these, but without party loyalty he can't make the voters stick to him while he fixes conventions, Is- nores platforms and does humorous things with the Declaration of Independence.
The politician uses party loyalty as a cement, but the voter usca it as a substitute for brains. If & voter has enough party loyalty he can get along without thinking at all. This is a great convenience for the voter, but it Is an even greater blessing for the politician who is using his party for revnue only. and who would be sadly both- ered if all the voters insisted on doing their own thinking before voting.
Party loyalty holds the par- ty together in adversity and enables it to sweep glori- ously to victo- y later on. Sometimes it sweeps to vic- tory like a cru- sader's vessel and sometimes like a pirate ship, but the man who is afflicted with party loyalty doesn't care how it eruleen, just so It gets there.
The man who has party loyalty in ita paresis state believes that no man in his own party can be bad and that no man in another party can be very good. He knows the difference between right and wrong, but believes that whatever wrong his party does is right. He hates wickedness and graft, but there is one greater ein than those not voting the straight ticket.
If we had lesa party loyalty in this country we could have a great deal more national loyalty, and the smooth grafters who edged into control of a party could have that party to themselves.
(Copyrighted by George Matthew Adainn.)

headlines.title: Story of the Moment
headlines.subtitle: A MISTAKEN POLICY. By Walt Mason.
headlines.fullText:
"I think the law should punish a man like young Mir. Trombone," remarked the landlady. "He was en- gaged to Sarah Wanderly and just when she was preparing for the marriage he left town and nobody knows where he is. There isn't a better girl in this town than Sarah, and the man who would treat her that way ought to be in the penitentiary."
"Barah truly Is all that you claim for her, Mrs. Jiggers," commented the star boarder. "She has so many admirable qualities that I have no doubt she would take the blue ribbon at the state fair, but even her warmest friends and admirers must admit she is drifting into the sere and yellow leaf. She has been engaged a number of times, and something al- ways turned up to sidetrack the nuptials.
"The trouble with Barah is that she has no reserve in the matter of her affections. She is entirely too candid, and lets her afflanced know that she simply can't get along without him. I remember when she and young Trombone came here to spend the even- ing about a month ago. She didn't realize that several highly praiseworthy gentlemen were seated in the room, making frantic efforts to maintain a poltte conver- sation. Had we been sitting there in our undercloth- ing it is doubtful whether she'd have noticed our bizarre apparel. Her eyes were glued upon Trom- bone, and she laughed at his feeblest witticlams and shed tears when he yawned.
"Until a girl is safely married, and the officiating clergyman has been paid off, a woman should never let her young man know that she considers him the one living issue in this world. She shouldn't let him think for a moment that he is necessary to her well being. Just as soon as the young man realizes that she thinks the sun rises and sets In his immediate vicinity, he loses interest in her. And we shouldn't point the finger of scorn at him, my dear Mrs. Jig- gers, for human nature is human nature the world over, and no man can properly appreciate the things which seem too cheap.
"A girl cheapena herself when she advertises her affection for any man. There was a period when I seriously contemplated making love to Sarah, myself. I was tired of eating boarding house doughnuts and codfish balls. No matter how skillfully such delica- cles are prepared, the most patient of boarders grows weary of them after a time, especially when he Is ex- pected to pay for them in advance. I had been told that Sarah was an excellent cook, and beautiful housekeeper, and for a few days I had visions of a happy home with that admirable woman presiding over the cookstove I even went so far as to buy a new suit of clothes, on time, in order that I might make a good impression when I called upon her.
"I made too good an impression. I saw at a glance that Sarah was more than willing to meet me half way, Her general behavior seemed to say, "You see, I am on the bargain counter, come and get me." I lost all interest in the matter at the outset. I pawned my new suit and reconciled myself to life imprison- ment in this family boarding house. Now, had Sarah shown the least disposition to shy when I approached her with matrimonial intentions, there can be no doubt that I'd have proposed to her after escorting her to the moving picture shows two or three times, and most likely we'de have been married by this time. And all unprejudiced people must admit that I am worth capturing. Her losing Sam Trombone was a trifling incident; but when she lost me it was a catastrophe.
"Sarah must change her tactics entirely if she hopes to win a husband. The fact that she wants to get married is notorious, and she must convince the marriageable public that the reverse is true, that only the greatest Inducements would make her con- sider the Idea of becoming a bride.
"The girls who are happily married, 3rm. Jiggers, are the ones who Impress upon their gentlemen friends their bettet that there are many pebbles on the beach; so many that any particular pebble docan't make any difference, And, since I have made this matter so plain that any child could understand it. I will ask you to favor me with a brief excerpt from the ple."

headlines.title: THERE YOU ARE.
headlines.fullText:
Elated at having at lant sold a picture to a dealer In curiosities by the way-Nugent Nochin decided to give an exhibition of his work. Ho arranged his ere- ations round the walls of his studio, and, having sent out about five thousand invitations awaited re- multa. On the first day in came an old lady, to see er if ma he non sold birthday cards, and an elderly man, who was wanting some- thing suitable for a pill advertisement.
Next day Nugent left his man-of-all work in charge and went out in search of Inspiration-and food. The man-of-all work, who was an aged mariner fallen en evil days, had a high opinion of his own capa- Butties and only awaited a chance of telling them to the world. His chance came when an old Indy presented herself and asked to see the pictures.
"Are you the artist?" she naked.
"Well, mum," answered the ancient mariner. "It's all according to wot you means by artist. That's my 'ead you see over there, and I'm in most of the other pictures, to0. 1 makes the frames and stretches the canvas; I washes the brushes, and I mixes the paint. Hall 'e does is put it on. And there you are!"-Pearson's Weekly.

headlines.title: SCOUTMASTER SCHOOL.
headlines.fullText:
An Interesting form of co-operation has been de- veloped by the Pittaburg counch and the University of Pittsburg in that etty.
Beginning on March 17, and lasting for eight weeks, there will be a night course for scouttasters at the university. Lectures will be given two evenings a week, Tuesday and Friday from 7:36 to 9:30, the first hour to be in charge of the university faculty and the second of scout headquarters.
The first four eveninits the university will give four lectures on adolescence, psychology and boy leadership. The next four on physical geography, elementary map drawing, and astronomy The scout head- quarters is to furnish Instructors for eight periods en nature study and enology. knot tying. sig- naling, "first aid" drills, hiking, fire-building, camp- Ing and camp cooking, etc.
Also a scout course is to be arranged in connec- tion with the regular summer school, and next fall there will be introduced into the university a regular course in scouting for which credit will be given- Rochester Post-Expresa.

headlines.title: HAY IN MIDDLE AGES.
headlines.fullText: Hay seems to have been little known in the mid- dle ages. Stock was wintered montly on straw and leaves. Tusser, who wrote in the reign of Queen Elizabeth, gives much advice to the farmers as to the cutting and storing of green branches, which might serve as fodder, when pasture failed. He Is particular as to the desirability ef saving the beech cuttings for the mlich cows; whereas for sheep any- thing would do. A miserable dependence It would appear to us now, but then it seems to have been looked on, if not as a matter of course, at least as a thing practiced by the more forehanded and capa- ble. There must have been some natural meadows in England, even then, but perhaps all their produce was saved for the horses of kings, As far as I can recollect, there is no mention of hay in Shakespeare, If we except Bottom's remark: "Good hay, sweet hay, hath no fellow." and even that suggests its rarity. By Milton's time, a hundred years later, "The mower whets his scythe." "And the tanned haycock in the mead" were familiar country objecta .- Farmer's Note Book

headlines.title: SOLICITUDE.
headlines.fullText:
"You don't know how I worry about my husband," said the tired-looking woman as she leaned un her broom for & moment.
"Why, there's nothing to worry about," answered the neighbor. "He's sitting in a chair en the back porch, fast asteep."
"Yes. But sometimes when I'm not there to look after him he's going to fall out of that chate and hurt himself."-Washington Star.

headlines.title: Abe Martin's Philosophy
headlines.fullText:
The trouble with a dry town is that ch' drug stores don't get the baseball scoren.
All the world loves a good lower

headlines.title: HIS IDEAL.
headlines.fullText:
"Why are you no pensive?" he asked.
"I'm not pensive," se she replied.
"But you haven't said a word for twenty minutes."
"Well, I didn't have anything to say."
"Don't you ever say anything when you have noth- ing to say?"
"No."
"Angel! Will you be my wife?"-St. Louis Past- Dispatch.

headlines.title: WANTED HIS SHARE.
headlines.fullText:
"Boss, won't yer help & poor man?"
"Bee here! I give you some money last week."
"Well, gee whist Ain't yer earned any more since?" -Boston Transcript.

headlines.title: PAT'S GREAT PLAN.
headlines.fullText:
During the preparation for battle Pat emerged from his tent wearing hin uniform hind side before. This soon attracted the attention of his superior officer, who demanded an explanation. Pat scratched hia head and seemed loath to reply, but finally replied with a sheepinh grin
"Well, begorta, Of thet if Of were it this way thin when Of had to retrate Of wouldn't get shot in the back."-Exchange,
